Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd usually wait until I felt like I absolutely had to do something, then I'd cram for a few hours. Sometimes I'd sit down with my laptop intending to study and end up just scrolling through other stuff because I couldn't focus. The 23 hours I put in each week were scattered and inconsistent, usually late at night when anxiety kicked in about falling behind.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then panic when a deadline was approaching and try to catch up all at once. It really depended on how stressed I was feeling or if I had other things going on in my life that seemed more pressing or just more interesting than coursework.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that made me log in. Like, I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or realize I hadn't checked the course in days and worry I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get a notification email that would remind me the LMS existed. It was rarely because I actually wanted to be there.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried a few times to make it a habit, like telling myself I'd check in every morning with my coffee, but it never stuck. The LMS just felt like another chore on my list. The mindfulness exercises they suggested actually helped my mood, but they didn't make me want to engage with the platform more.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I could watch them while doing other things, like stretching or moving around. I'm a hands-on learner, so just reading stuff doesn't work for me. When there were practical examples or demonstrations in the videos, I actually retained something.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums entirely. I only posted once because it was required. Reading other people's comments felt tedious and I never felt like I had anything valuable to add. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because they seemed optional and I was already overwhelmed with the required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate payoff, like it directly connected to an assignment I needed to submit. If something felt abstract or like busy work, I'd tune out completely. I needed to feel like what I was doing would actually matter for my grade or help me understand something specific.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't going to affect my grade, I just didn't have the motivation to do it. Required ones at least had some external pressure pushing me forward. Without that pressure, I'd drift toward literally anything else.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of the PDFs and videos so I could study offline. I'd take notes by hand sometimes because writing things out helps me remember better than typing. I also used YouTube to find explanations of concepts that the course materials didn't make clear enough for me.
Q10: There were definitely times I was studying from printed notes or rewatching downloaded videos on my tablet without being connected to anything. I'd also pace around my room talking through concepts out loud, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere. My best learning happens when I'm moving, not sitting at a screen.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video, watch it once online, then rewatch it two or three more times offline while doing other things. The LMS would only show that one initial access, but I'd actually spent way more time with the material than it looked like.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof and I just felt paralyzed. Everything seemed overwhelming and I couldn't make myself care about the coursework. I was dealing with some personal stuff that made it hard to concentrate, and the boredom with the material made it even easier to check out.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed about other things, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. I'd log in, stare at the screen, feel overwhelmed by everything I hadn't done, and then just close it. Fatigue made it impossible to focus, and competing responsibilities always won because coursework felt so abstract.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but I'd be completely zoned out, thinking about something else entirely. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually absorbing any of it. My eyes were on the screen but my brain was somewhere else. It was just going through the motions.
Q15: I avoided the bigger assignments until the last possible moment because they stressed me out so much. The anxiety about doing them poorly made me put them off, which then made the anxiety worse. It was this awful cycle. I knew they were important but facing them felt impossible until the deadline forced my hand.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, then go quiet again. Without a deadline looming, I had zero urgency. The structure of having due dates was the only external force keeping me somewhat on track.
Q17: When I got good feedback on an assignment, it gave me a brief boost, like maybe I wasn't completely failing. But honestly, that feeling faded quickly. Negative feedback just confirmed my fears that I wasn't cut out for this, which made me want to engage even less. The feedback didn't really change my habits much.
Q18: The course was structured in a way that gave me too much freedom, I think. With 18 online courses to manage, I needed more guidance on what to prioritize. The open structure meant I could easily fall behind without anyone noticing until it was too late. More check-ins might have helped me stay accountable.
Q19: The lack of interactive or hands-on elements made engagement harder for me. I learn by doing, not by reading or watching passively. When there were practical exercises, I actually got into it. But most of the course was just consuming content, which made me zone out constantly.
Q20: I think some students might be like me, where they're logging in and clicking around but not actually absorbing anything. You can spend hours on the LMS just opening things, getting overwhelmed, and closing them again without producing anything. High activity doesn't mean high engagement or understanding.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I did okay on exams and assignments despite not being super active online because I studied in ways that didn't show up. I'd cram effectively when I needed to, use external resources, and my kinesthetic learning happened offline. The LMS activity didn't reflect my actual effort.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the anxiety that keeps you from engaging even when you're logged in. They capture behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when you're curious and invested, not just doing the minimum to avoid failing. I wasn't really engaged this semester—I was just surviving and trying to manage my anxiety.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped my mood even if it didn't change my study habits. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent pacing and talking through concepts, or the genuine learning that happened away from the screen. It's just numbers without context.
